edibility.preparation: Young leaves in salad; roots roasted as coffee substitute
edibility.hazards: Bitter when mature; latex may irritate

edibility.preparation: Blanch or dry to neutralize stinging hairs; excellent soup/puree
edibility.hazards: Stinging hairs on raw contact; harvest with gloves

lookalikes.name: Wood nettle (Laportea canadensis)
lookalikes.note: Not common in CA; similar sting

edibility.preparation: All parts edible; garlicky flavor; invasive — harvest freely
edibility.hazards: None significant when smell confirms Allium

lookalikes.name: Death camas (Toxicoscordion venenosum)
lookalikes.note: NO onion/garlic smell — if it doesn't smell like onion, do not eat

edibility.preparation: Berries MUST be cooked; flowers for fritters/tea when fully open
edibility.hazards: Raw berries, bark, leaves, and unripe fruit are toxic (cyanogenic glycosides)

lookalikes.name: Red elderberry (Sambucus racemosa)
lookalikes.note: Red clusters; also requires cooking; less desirable

edibility.hazards: Confusable with poison hemlock — MUST confirm by crushing and smelling anise/licorice

lookalikes.name: Poison hemlock (Conium maculatum)
lookalikes.note: NO anise smell; purple-spotted stems; grows in same habitats — fatal if misidentified

edibility.preparation: Best native blackberry; ripe when deep purple-black

lookalikes.name: Rubus armeniacus (Himalayan blackberry)
lookalikes.note: Larger, more aggressive invasive; both edible

edibility.preparation: Raw in salads; mild, succulent; classic CA forager green
Use 1/2 leaf to flavor soups — whole leaf is overpowering and can cause headache
edibility.hazards: Concentrated leaf oil is toxic; never eat large quantities

lookalikes.name: Bay laurel (Laurus nobilis)
lookalikes.note: Cultivated culinary bay; similar use as seasoning only

edibility.preparation: Young leaves cooked like spinach; mucilaginous texture

lookalikes.name: Other mallow species
lookalikes.note: Most Malva spp. are similarly edible
